text: What is the minimum number of people needed in a room so the probability of at least two sharing a birthday exceeds 50%? (365 days, equally likely.)
explanation: Birthday Paradox. P(all different with n people) = 365!/((365−n)!×365ⁿ). At n=23: P(match) ≈ 50.7%. With 23 people there are 253 pairs — enough for >50% chance.

text: Three boxes: one has 2 gold coins, one has 2 silver, one has 1 gold + 1 silver. You pick a box randomly and draw one coin — it's gold. Probability the other coin is also gold?
explanation: Bertrand's Box. 3 gold coins exist: Gold₁, Gold₂ (box 1), Gold₃ (mixed box). Drawing gold → equally likely to be any of these 3. If Gold₁: other is Gold₂ ✓. If Gold₂: other is Gold₁ ✓. If Gold₃: other is Silver ✗. P = 2/3.

text: A family has exactly two children. Given at least one is a boy, what's the probability both are boys? (Equal likelihood.)
explanation: Sample space: {BB, BG, GB, GG}. 'At least one boy' eliminates GG → {BB, BG, GB}. P(BB) = 1/3. Note: if 'the OLDER child is a boy' → P = 1/2 (only {BB, BG}).

text: A fair coin has landed heads 10 times in a row. What is the probability the next flip is heads?
explanation: Gambler's Fallacy. Each flip is independent — the coin has no memory. P(next heads) = 1/2 always. 1/1024 is the probability of 11 heads IN A ROW prospectively, not the conditional probability.
